summary: Gruber responds to Joe Wilcox and Dan Lyons's criticisms about his characterization of the Apple-Google rivalry. He argues that the issue isn't that Google entered the phone business — they bought Android in 2005 — but that Google fundamentally pivoted Android from a BlackBerry-style competitor into a direct iPhone competitor after seeing Apple's product. He contends this pivot justified Apple's retaliatory moves like iAds and blocking Google's analytics. In a separate thread, he defends his long-standing decision not to have comments on Daring Fireball, arguing that comments on popular sites are shouting matches, not conversations, and that his curated, no-comments format is what makes DF effective.
key_points: Google's Android pivot from BlackBerry-style designs to iPhone-style touchscreen devices is the core grievance, not Google entering mobile per se
Apple's retaliatory moves (iAds, blocking AdMob analytics) were predictable consequences of Google repositioning Android as a direct iPhone competitor
Google's strategy was a 'heads we win, tails we still win' play on mobile advertising that Apple refused to accept
Gruber defends his no-comments policy as intentional curation rather than a deficiency, arguing popular site comments are cacophonous shouting matches
Android may prove a long-term financial mistake for Google by turning a former ally with a rich advertising platform into an enemy
money_quotes: It's not that Google changed course and got into the phone business, period. It's that they got into the iPhone's segment of the phone business.

What's goofy is the idea that Google would do this — to aggressively change Android from a BlackBerry/Windows Mobile competitor into an iPhone competitor — and that anyone would expect Apple not to retaliate.
The difference with Apple isn't that they're the nice guys who won. The difference with Apple is that they're the first winners with taste.
I don't care what's out of place. I care about what's best.
What makes DF an efficient and effective soapbox is exactly that it is not noisy. My goal is for not a single wasted word to appear anywhere on any page of the site.
Comments, at least on popular websites, aren't conversations. They're cacophonous shouting matches. DF is a curated conversation, to be sure, but that's the whole premise.
Android may wind up a long-term mistake for Google, financially.
Is my soapbox bigger than Joe Wilcox's? Yes it is. But that's fair, because I built this soapbox myself.
key_insight: Google's pivoting Android from a BlackBerry competitor into an iPhone clone was a competitive provocation that made Apple's retaliatory moves not just predictable but inevitable — and may ultimately cost Google more than it gains.
